Bus

Homepage Bus Bus Inside Streetcar Streetcar Inside Subway Subway Inside
Hey, thanks for choosing the bus, swipe through, and learn something new. Firstly, the bus stops at every major subway stop or intersection, so finding a bus stop should be easy-peasy. Look out for shelters and signage with the 2-digit number like 19 Bay. Buses should come every 10 minutes, day and night. Most routes operate from 6 a.m. to 1 a.m. or 2 a.m., with the "Blue Night Network" running every 30 minutes or better after that. There are also shuttle buses that run when the other transit systems aren't operating; they usually follow the same routes as the regular buses, but their routes may change and not take you exactly where you want to go. Always look and listen for the final bus stop.
The commission is adding bus lanes, which will help speed up bus times. Great for accessibility and safer because the bus is next to the sidewalk, not in the middle of the road (Streetcar), and you don’t have to worry about crazy drivers. If you’re travelling to a smaller area, the bus is your only option to get to major areas.
During rush hour, it's a nightmare. Since the bus is smaller, riders have to squeeze together, push, and shove to get in. So, not only do you have to deal with the crowd, but with the bus,you also have to deal with traffic, so you’re stuck on a crowded bus for a longer time.
If it’s cold outside, hope you dressed warmly, because you’ll be waiting longer, and the bus shelter will keep you cold, so always check the schedule.
List of strategies before getting on:

1. Always have your way of payment on hand (Presto, Coins, Tickets, Pass)

2. Stand close to the stop, look at the bus, and act like you want to get on, because they might not stop. This is rare, but it has happened to me

3. Look at the lit-up bus route at the top and make sure it’s the right bus

4. Finally, before getting on, allow others to get off first.
